title: How to Use Psychometry to Read Objects
meta_description: Unlock the hidden stories in everyday objects. Learn psychometry, the psychic skill you already have. Start reading energy now.
og_title: How to Use Psychometry to Read Objects
og_description: Unlock the hidden stories in everyday objects. Learn psychometry, the psychic skill you already have. Start reading energy now.
body:
You Can Read Objects. Here's How.

You've probably done this before. You pick up a friend's ring or a book from a thrift store, and suddenly you feel something. A heaviness. A flicker of sadness. A weird sense of comfort that doesn't belong to you. You shake it off, tell yourself it's your imagination, and move on.

Stop shaking it off.

That feeling? That's not your imagination. That's your nervous system picking up on information that's stored in the object itself. It's real. And you can learn to work with it on purpose instead of having it ambush you in the middle of a flea market.

This is psychometry. It's the ability to "read" the energy, history, and emotional residue stored in physical objects. And before you think this is some kind of rare gift reserved for psychic mediums on TV - it's not. You already have this ability. You've just been taught to ignore it.

"Objects hold memory. Not in the way a photograph holds an image, but in the way a riverbed holds the shape of water that passed through it. You don't need to be special to feel it. You just need to stop pretending you don't."

What Psychometry Actually Is (And Isn't)

Let's clear something up right away. Psychometry isn't magic. It's not about "magically" knowing someone's secrets or predicting the future from a watch. It's about sensitivity. Objects absorb energy from the people who handle them, the environments they've lived in, and the emotions that played out around them. You're not reading the object like a book. You're feeling the residue that's still clinging to it.

Think of it like this: if you walk into a room where two people just had a screaming fight, you feel it. The air is thick. Your shoulders tighten. You don't know what happened, but your body knows something's off. Objects work the same way. A wedding ring worn for thirty years carries a different charge than a keychain from a gas station. Your body can tell the difference. You just have to pay attention.

I've been teaching this stuff for years, and the number one thing people tell me is, "But I'm not psychic." And I always say the same thing: you don't have to be. You just have to be willing to feel what's already there. That's it. That's the whole thing.

Does that land? Good. Let's get into the practical stuff.

Why Your Brain Will Fight You On This

Here's the thing you need to understand before you try anything: your brain is going to tell you you're making it up. It's going to narrate, analyze, and explain away every single sensation you get. "That's just static electricity." "That's just because you saw a movie about a haunted locket once." "You're just tired."

That voice is not the truth. That voice is your brain trying to keep you safe by keeping you in the world it understands. The rational, logical, "everything has a physical explanation" world. And that world is real. But it's not the only one.

You have to learn to let that voice talk while you keep working anyway. Don't try to shut it up - that'll just make it louder. Just say, "Thanks, brain. I hear you. Now let me feel what I feel." And then do it.

Your First Practice Object

Don't grab a family heirloom or something that belongs to someone you're emotionally tangled with. You want something neutral. A rock from your backyard. A spoon from a diner. A key you found on the ground. Something that hasn't been soaked in strong emotional energy, because you need to learn what a "quiet" object feels like first.

Hold it in your non-dominant hand. Your left hand if you're right-handed, right hand if you're left-handed. That hand is more connected to your receptive, intuitive side. Your dominant hand is for doing. Your non-dominant hand is for receiving.

Close your eyes. Don't try to "get" anything. Just notice what your hand feels. Temperature. Texture. Pressure. Weight. Does the object feel warm in some spots and cool in others? Does it feel heavier than it should? Lighter? These are the physical sensations that carry the energetic information.

Stay with it for two minutes. That's it. Two minutes of just feeling. If your mind wanders, bring it back to your hand. Don't judge what you notice. Just notice.

What You're Actually Feeling

Once you've practiced with a few neutral objects, you'll start to notice patterns. A rock from a riverbed might feel cool and still. A coin from a busy city might feel buzzing, almost agitated. A piece of clothing from a thrift store might feel heavy or sad, especially if it belonged to someone who passed away.

These aren't random. They're the emotional and environmental residue I mentioned earlier. Think of it like scent. A shirt that's been in a smoky room smells like smoke even after the smoke is gone. An object that's been in a room full of grief carries grief. An object that's been handled with love carries warmth.

You're not "reading" the object. You're letting the object read you. It transmits information through your hand, and your body translates that information into sensation. Temperature, pressure, emotion, even images or words that pop into your head. That's all valid data.

Here's a simple approach to make sense of what you're feeling:

  • Temperature changes - warmth often means positive or active energy, coolness can mean neutral or stored/old energy
  • Tingling or buzzing - the object has a lot of emotional charge or has been handled by someone with strong emotions
  • Heaviness - grief, depression, trauma, or just a lot of history
  • Lightness - joy, peace, or an object that hasn't been deeply connected to anyone
  • Sudden emotions - you feel sad, angry, or happy for no reason - that's not yours
  • Images or words - your mind might show you a flash of a face, a place, or a name. Don't force it. Let it come.

None of this is right or wrong. It's just data. The more you practice, the more you'll learn your own "language" of sensation. What feels like sadness to you might feel like pressure to someone else. That's fine. You're not trying to be objective. You're trying to be honest about what you're experiencing.

How to Read an Object for Someone Else

This is where it gets interesting. Someone hands you an object and says, "What do you feel?" And you have to actually deliver something useful. No pressure, right?

First, set a boundary. You're not a mind reader. You're a feeler. You're not going to know their grandmother's middle name or what they had for breakfast. You're going to feel the emotional weather of the object. That's it.

Hold the object in your non-dominant hand. Take three slow breaths. Say to yourself (silently or out loud), "I'm open to whatever this object wants to share. I don't need to be right. I just need to be present."

Then wait. Don't force anything. If nothing happens for thirty seconds, that's fine. Sometimes objects are quiet. Sometimes they're guarded. Sometimes you just need more time. Say what you feel, even if it's "I'm not getting much right now. It feels quiet. Peaceful, maybe." That's valid.

If you do get something, say it simply. "I'm feeling a heaviness in my chest. Like there's sadness here. Not fresh sadness - old sadness. Like something that happened a long time ago." Don't add interpretation. Just report what you're feeling. Let the other person make sense of it.

You'll be surprised how often they say, "That's exactly right." Because it is. You're not guessing. You're feeling what's there.

A Note About Skepticism

Some people will hand you an object and say, "Prove it." Don't play that game. You don't have to prove anything. Psychometry isn't a party trick. It's a real skill that requires trust and openness. If someone is testing you, they're not in a receptive state. Their skepticism will block your reception. That's not your fault. Just say, "This works best when we're both open. If you're not, that's okay. Maybe another time."

And honestly? Sometimes you'll be wrong. You'll feel something that doesn't match anything the owner knows. That doesn't mean you failed. It could mean you're picking up on something they don't know about - an earlier owner, a past event, something that happened before they got the object. Or it could mean you're projecting your own stuff onto it. That happens. You learn to clean your own energy so you don't mix it up with what you're reading.

If you want to get serious about this, there are great books that can help you build your skills. See on Amazon (paid link) is a solid resource for understanding how energy attaches to objects and how to work with that sensitivity without burning out.

How to Protect Yourself When Reading Objects

This is the part nobody talks about because it sounds "woo-woo." But it's practical. If you're sensitive, picking up a grieving person's necklace can leave you feeling heavy for hours. You need a way to let that go.

Before you pick up an object, set an intention. Say (out loud or silently), "I'm open to receiving information from this object, but I'm not taking on its energy. I can feel it without owning it." That's a boundary. Your nervous system will listen.

After you're done, shake out your hands. Run them under cool water. Touch something alive - a plant, soil, your own skin. Take three deep breaths. Imagine the energy you picked up flowing out of your hands and into the earth. You don't need to keep it. It's not yours.

If you're reading objects regularly, you'll want to ground yourself daily. Walk barefoot on grass. Eat something hearty. Laugh. Do things that remind your body it's here, in this world, not floating in everyone else's emotional soup.

Another thing that helps is having a "clearing" object of your own. A stone or a piece of jewelry that you never let anyone else touch. You hold it after a reading to reset your energy. It becomes an anchor for your own frequency.

And if you're looking for a deeper practice around energy protection and clearing, See on Amazon (paid link) has some excellent techniques that are practical and not overly spiritual. It's about boundaries, not magic.

Reading Objects in the Wild

Once you've practiced at home, take it out into the world. Thrift stores are incredible for this. They're full of objects with unknown histories, and nobody's watching you expectantly. You can just wander and feel.

Walk through the store with your hand hovering a few inches above shelves. Notice where you feel a pull. A warmth. A strange urge to pick something up. That's your intuition guiding you to an object that has something to say.

Hold it. Feel it. See what comes. You don't have to buy it. You're just practicing. If you do get a strong hit, you can buy it and work with it more at home. But mostly, you're training your sensitivity in a low-stakes environment.

Antique stores are even better. Those objects have been handled by generations. They carry layers of history. You might pick up a teacup and feel a Victorian parlor, a woman's loneliness, a child's birthday party. All of it is there. You just have to let it in.

Flea markets and estate sales are also goldmines. Just be aware that the emotional residue can be intense, especially if the objects belonged to someone who recently passed. Pace yourself. Don't try to read ten things in a row. Your nervous system will get overwhelmed.

What to Do With What You Get

Sometimes you'll get information that feels important. A name. A place. A sense of how someone died. What do you do with that? Most of the time, nothing. You're not a detective. You're not obligated to track down the owner's family and tell them their grandmother's brooch says she was happy. You're just practicing a skill. Let the information pass through you. You don't have to act on it.

If you're reading for someone else, share what you get with gentleness. "I'm feeling like this object has seen a lot of change. Maybe multiple moves or a big life transition." That's safe. That's respectful. You don't need to say, "I sense a divorce and a dog that died." Unless they ask for that level of detail, keep it broad.

Remember: you're not here to be impressive. You're here to be honest. And honesty is often quieter than we think.

Your Sensitivity Is Not a Flaw

If you've made it this far, you're probably someone who's been told your whole life that you're "too sensitive." That you feel things too much. That you need to toughen up. That you're imagining things.

You're not imagining things. You're feeling what's actually there. Most people have learned to numb out that sensitivity because it's overwhelming. You haven't been able to. That's not a weakness. That's the foundation of a skill that most people will never develop.

Psychometry is just one way to use that sensitivity. Once you learn to trust it with objects, you'll start noticing it everywhere. You'll walk into a room and know the emotional temperature. You'll meet someone and feel their history in a handshake. You'll pick up a book and know if it was loved or abandoned.

That's not weird. That's awake.

And if you want to go deeper into understanding your own sensitivity and how to work with it without getting drained, See on Amazon (paid link) is a great next step. It talks directly to people who feel too much and don't know what to do with it.

A Simple Practice to Start Today

You don't need a teacher or a class to start. You need one object and five minutes. Here's a practice you can do right now.

Find something in your pocket, your bag, or your desk. A pen. A key. A coin. Hold it in your non-dominant hand. Close your eyes. Breathe. For the first minute, just feel the physical sensations. Temperature, texture, weight. For the second minute, ask silently: "What do you want me to know?" Don't force an answer. Just stay open. For the third minute, notice if anything has changed. Does the object feel different than it did at the start? Do you feel different?

Write down what you noticed. Even if it's "nothing." That's data. Tomorrow, do it again with a different object. In a week, you'll start to see patterns. In a month, you'll wonder how you ever walked through the world without feeling what's really there.

That's it. That's the practice. No secrets. No special initiation. Just showing up and feeling what's already there.

You've got this. You've always had it. You just had to be told it was real.

If you want to test your sensitivity and see where you're at, take the quick assessment at intuitivesawaken.com/assessment or try the intuitivesawaken.com/quiz to find out what kind of intuitive you are. It'll give you a clearer picture of your natural strengths and where to focus your practice next.
